item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just discovered this, and as the author of 2315, was surprised by the connection. I still dig it, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good and Im not surprised, you've written some of my favorite stuff on the site
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first three words to pop into my head after reading that last line on the image where "what", "the", and "hell". At first it seems like some sort of entity simply trying to get rid of infectious SCPs, then it talks like a guy who wears a hospital gown and tissue boxes on his feet at home. Bravo, this is one of the most unnerving things I've seen in a good while now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll gie a hand with it. You'll hear from me in the next few days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked this as a draft and I enjoyed the final product. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: extremely goddamn good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll put that on the to-do list. Directorate K is dreadfully under-utilized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is neat, funny and drinks a lot from Kalinin's work. Also, you really need to further a possible large scale war between DirK and HEALTH.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wha? Is the UN the other one of the both?

That's an artifact from when there used to be a second organization there. Fixed.

Fucking with the kerning is not even close to worth my time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great. Although I'll echo that some of the cross-links could be pruned. And:

SCP-3404-C has detailed knowledge of the Foundation, and treats both as hostile forces.

Wha? Is the UN the other one of the both?

Also, I could tell that the last pic was made in Paint, which took a nibble out of the plausibility. You pattern-matched the general look of spammy local-mom internet ads pretty well, but they tend to have crisper anti-aliasing on the text. Edit: wait, no, is it GIMP? I'd recognise that weird kerning anywhere

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Went through this again, a little more slowly. Caught the reference to Directorate K, and wished I could upvote again.

This makes me think of what some singularitarians imagine AI being like*. Intelligent, self-aware, abhuman. All we can know of its nature is the observed output, which doesn't track to our nature in an significant way.

Now that I think about it, the Foundationverse has most of the more convincing depictions of nonhuman intelligence that I've read lately.

*So glad I don't have to diagram that sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this definitely explains the music of HEALTH.

Which is great by the way. Everyone should go watch Die Slow on YouTube.

YOU WILL LOVE EACH OTHER YOU WILL LOVE EACH OTHER YOU WILL LOVE EACH OTHER YOU WILL LOVE EACH OTHER YOU WILL LOVE EACH OTHER YOU WILL LOVE EACH OTHER

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• Crosslinks addressed above; take it as you will. You're completely right about the GOC — I had them in there because they felt like they should be. But that's a bad way to go about writing. Removed them.
  • Good call. Fixed to be clearer.
  • Humaniform means "Like a human or that of a human in form or appearance", according to Wiktionary; "Humaniform intelligence" should imply "brain that works and thinks like a human brain". You're absolutely right that, as a gestalt, it's very much not human — as the article states, "SCP-3404-C is not a humaniform intelligence"
  • 4117's exact nature isn't important to the article. Truthfully, I have vague plans to actually write that article (sneak preview: the theme will be 'heart surgery') and then change 4117 to whatever number it is. But all that the reader needs to know is: 3404-C made SCP-4117 and used them as test subjects, then tried repeatedly to take them back from the Foundation.
  • The article definitely characterizes it as deranged, but I'd argue that it doesn't come off as truly angry until that last line. Fearful and willing to fight, maybe, but as an emotional response I don't think anything else in the article really signals "angry".

Glad you enjoyed the article, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2315 was, admittedly, me trying to pick something very weird and unexpected. Like all the other stuff there should be some fairly self-evident connection to the topic at hand, not to mention I can only milk medical stuff specifically so much before the article gets monotonous. So I browsed the 'neurological' tag until I found something that had no apparent explanation that fits the timeline and was sufficiently weird.

… that said, these issues do sorta force me to tip my hand a bit. While it's meant to stand on its own merits regardless, the early stages of brainstorming for Operation HEALTH had it as a GOI. I ultimately didn't like having a big group of fucked up germophobes appear for no reason so I wrote this SCP as an origin story, and then I couldn't think of something to give them an edge & get them together until I thought "HIVE MIND" and at that point I couldn't properly call it a GOI any more than Directorate K is a GOI. But I still wanted this to be viable as a springboard for future writing of myself and others — a GOI in all but name. Not like I haven't made my mark on that list already, lol.

So, that underpins a lot of the crosslinking and redaction. The former because I wanted to make it as clear as possible that 3404-C knows about and has a stake in the anomalous world and is properly embedded in the Foundationverse1; the latter because I wanted to make it clear that the whole story of SCP-3404 is not contained in this document, and that there's room for other pieces (or other authors) to work.

The redactions are also supposed to serve as speculation fuel — given what we know about 3404-C, and what we know of what it does, what else could it be doing that the reader isn't supposed to know? A lot of the event log was based around that — stuff that serves to leave people intrigued without providing too many answers, mixed in with stuff whose purpose is perfectly obvious.

I can understand how the redactions and crosslinks could potentially be offputting, but I judged they were necessary to evoke the feelings I wanted and situate the article where I wanted it. I'm open to more thematically appropriate suggestions to put over 2315 (following the criteria in the first paragraph), but I do prefer that they continue to be Very Weird.

Footnotes
1. Also 008/500/610 are great motivators for 3404-C that require no introduction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a few critiques:

  • Few too many crosslinks were used here when a lot were probably not necessary. The GOC also pops up here for zero reason.
  • I have no idea what the first sentence of the description means. The anomaly refers to one part of the anomaly but not the other two?
  • What does humaniform intelligence even mean? And wouldn't the fact that 3404-C being a gestalt intelligence sort of imply that it's anything but human?
  • I do like how you're setting up a canon with 4117 there, but it's not very useful in determining the quality of this article when we don't really know what it is.
  • The whole 'give me back my fucking test subjects' bit comes off as slightly forced to show how angry this thing is. I already got the impression it was deranged.

Other than that, really enjoyed. Artificial intelligence that tries to do what's best for humanity but ends up screwing up and making things worse is one of my favorite tropes, and the writing is spectacular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really interesting, but I found some of the crosslinks distracting/excessive. The log entry about creating 2315 was really unexpected to the point of feeling like a non-sequitur, as I figured it would be something biological or medical, or otherwise related in theme,. Also, I feel like the redactions in the log were a bit excessive and didn't really add much.

+1 anyway, it's very cohesive and I liked the method of using 4117 as a placeholder so that the reader knows you're talking about the same SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was quite fun. It was like the base concept of 49 (anomaly trying to cure humanity via morally ambiguous methods) flipped on its head several times and actually done justice.

item.title: Give me back my fucking test subjects.
item.content:encoded:
As far as ways to end the world and/or breach the masquerade go, this is a simple and effective one that spins out the logical consequences of the initial event in a really entertaining way. I genuinely burst out into a grin at "Began funding the construction of several super-deep boreholes off the coast of Mozambique" and ""an effective solvent" on sheep": the final image that caps off the piece is a good example of seeming wordvomit with an internal logic behind it.

Entertaining and just fun as hell to read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Living Dead Girl by Rob Zombie provided inspiration by way of some misheard lyrics (in the original, it's "operation filth"), and I listened to it on repeat while writing for mood music.

Big thanks to everyone who gave feedback!

Give me back my fucking test subjects
